spec_match.rationale: Uses document.activeViewTransition exactly the way the spec motivates: a router checks the property and short-circuits a racing call instead of letting startViewTransition stomp the in-flight one.

interactive.rationale: Real router with four route buttons, live support readout, currently-transitioning readout, and an event log — clicking and double-clicking exercises the guard path directly.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index blurb sells 'a miniature router uses activeViewTransition to drop racing route clicks' and the page implements exactly that.

edge_cases.rationale: Happy path (guard skips racing click) is covered; missing: queueing strategy (drop vs latest-wins), what happens when popstate fires mid-transition, and behaviour when startViewTransition itself is absent but activeViewTransition is supported (theoretically impossible but worth a probe).

references.rationale: 'See also' lists both chromestatus.com/feature/5067126381215744 and the activeViewTransition spec anchor.

design_system.rationale: All colours via CSS variables, button.active override sets both background AND color (per design-system rule 6), no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Add a latest-wins queueing strategy alongside drop
openQuestions.detail: Real SPA routers often prefer the most recent click rather than dropping it — they want to queue the navigation, let the current transition finish, then run the queued one. The page currently only shows the 'drop' strategy. Add a toggle for 'drop vs queue latest' so authors see both common patterns and the activeViewTransition + finished.then() coordination required for queueing.

openQuestions.title: Show a popstate / back-button race
openQuestions.detail: The hard case for routers is not 'user clicks twice' but 'user clicks then hits browser back' — popstate fires from a different code path. Add a 'simulate back' button that fires a popstate-equivalent navigation while the transition is in flight, and demonstrate the guard catching that path too.

openQuestions.title: Compare with/without the guard side-by-side
openQuestions.detail: The blurb sells 'without a guard, the second transition cancels the first mid-flight and the UI flickers' but the page never shows the unguarded failure mode. A 'disable guard' toggle that re-enables stomping would make the contrast visible and teach why the property exists.

summary: The headline use case of the feature, executed cleanly. The guard pattern is real and the router works. To go deeper, show the failure mode it prevents (toggleable guard) and add a queue-latest alternative so the comparison is two routers, not one.
